title: Johnson & Johnson — Immunology — Synthesis

frontmatter.sources: web research + knowledge doc + ZoomRx project footprint

html:
J&J Immunology Cross-Study Synthesis

Generated: 2026-04-26 Sources: ZoomRx project footprint (KAM Tracking, Ferma Congress, FRM Tracking, MSL Tracking), J&J Q1 2026 earnings, AbbVie competitive data, ICOTYDE launch data Method: Pattern synthesis across ZoomRx immunology engagement history and market dynamics

1. Overview

TREMFYA is J&J's most important growth asset in immunology — a $1.6B/quarter franchise growing 74% YoY. But it operates in the shadow of Skyrizi ($17.3B annualized), is sharing its derm base with the newly launched ICOTYDE, and must absorb the Stelara biosimilar erosion that pulled immunology segment revenue down 8.8% in Q1 2026. The strategic question for ZoomRx: Where does tracking insight create the most commercial leverage for a product that's winning on execution but trailing on data?

2. Cross-Study Patterns

Pattern 1: TREMFYA Is Winning New Starts in GI Despite Skyrizi's Data Advantage

The most commercially significant signal in immunology right now. TREMFYA is the new-start share leader in IBD (Q1 2026 earnings), despite Skyrizi holding a 75% capture rate among IL-23 drugs in frontline IBD and the SEQUENCE head-to-head win in Crohn's.

What this means: J&J's field force execution — MSL engagement, KAM relationships, formulary access work — is overcoming Skyrizi's data moat in IBD. This is a testable hypothesis: ZoomRx tracking data should show whether TREMFYA's SOV leadership translates to prescribing preference, or whether new-start share is being driven by access/formulary mechanics rather than physician conviction.

Implication for ZoomRx: A GI-specific KAM/MSL tracking study that disentangles SOV vs. formulary access vs. data-driven preference would be highly valuable to J&J. The "why are we winning despite inferior data?" question is one only primary research can answer.

Pattern 2: ICOTYDE Launch Creates a Dual-Brand Messaging Challenge in Derm

ICOTYDE (oral IL-23, approved March 18, 2026) launched with "strong demand" and drove a $300M outlook raise. J&J now has two IL-23-based psoriasis products with fundamentally different modalities: injectable (TREMFYA) and oral (ICOTYDE). The messaging tension: how do reps position two same-mechanism, same-company products without cannibalizing the higher-revenue asset?

What this means: ZoomRx KAM and Ferma Congress tracking should show early signals of:

  • Dermatologist awareness of ICOTYDE vs. TREMFYA differentiation
  • Whether reps lead with ICOTYDE (oral convenience) or TREMFYA (multi-indication breadth)
  • Patient switching patterns from TREMFYA to ICOTYDE in psoriasis
  • Competitive response from Skyrizi reps exploiting the "confused portfolio" narrative

Implication for ZoomRx: ICOTYDE launch tracking is a natural extension of existing TREMFYA tracking. The dual-brand dynamic creates a unique research opportunity — few pharma companies have two products targeting the same pathway in the same indication.

Pattern 3: Stelara Biosimilar Erosion Is Creating a Massive Sales Force Pivot

Stelara was J&J's largest drug ($10B+ in 2024). Biosimilar erosion has already pulled immunology revenue down 8.8% in Q1 2026. As Stelara revenue declines, J&J is redeploying sales force capacity toward TREMFYA and ICOTYDE — the largest field force restructuring in J&J immunology history.

What this means: FRM tracking should capture:

  • Rep messaging quality during the Stelara-to-TREMFYA pivot
  • Whether Stelara-experienced reps can effectively sell a GI story (different prescriber universe)
  • Access/formulary dynamics as payers gain a cheaper biosimilar option (does Stelara biosimilar availability affect TREMFYA tier positioning?)

Pattern 4: IL-23 Class Expansion Is a Rising Tide — But Not Equal for All Boats

The IL-23 class has gone from 5% to 40% penetration in UC within one year. This unprecedented class expansion benefits both Skyrizi and TREMFYA — but market share within the class is where the competitive battle is fought.

What this means: ZoomRx should track not just brand-level metrics but class-level dynamics:

  • Is IL-23 class growth driven by new-to-biologic patients or TNF/JAK switchers?
  • What is the GI physician's mental model of IL-23 agents — class effect or brand-specific?
  • How do formulary decisions (class-level preferred agent) affect individual brand uptake?

Pattern 5: GI MSL Engagement Is J&J's Competitive Moat — But Quality May Lag SOV

ZoomRx tracking data shows J&J MSLs lead on SOV and reach in GI vs. competitors. But Skyrizi slightly outperforms on SDP effectiveness — suggesting that high-frequency engagement is not converting to perceived clinical differentiation.

What this means: The SOV-to-effectiveness gap is the most actionable research finding for J&J's MSL team. Questions for future tracking:

  • What content are J&J MSLs delivering vs. AbbVie MSLs?
  • Are J&J MSLs discussing TREMFYA UC and CD data with equal comfort?
  • Does the Stelara biosimilar narrative complicate MSL credibility (defending the franchise vs. promoting the successor)?

3. What's Missing

Gap 1: No Head-to-Head TREMFYA vs. Skyrizi Tracking in GI

The most important competitive dynamic in immunology is unmeasured by ZoomRx. Physician perception of TREMFYA vs. Skyrizi comparative effectiveness in IBD — outside of clinical trial data — would be enormously valuable.

Gap 2: No ICOTYDE Launch Tracking

ICOTYDE launched March 2026. ZoomRx has no ICOTYDE-specific tracking in field. The dual-brand dynamic, dermatologist awareness, and cannibalization signal are all unmeasured.

Gap 3: No Cross-Sub-TA Tracking

TREMFYA spans derm, GI, and rheum — three different prescriber universes with different competitive sets. ZoomRx tracking may be siloed by sub-TA rather than tracking the cross-domain experience of multi-specialty prescribers.

Gap 4: No Patient Experience in GI

All tracking is HCP-perspective. Patient experience with TREMFYA in UC/CD (infusion vs. SubQ, symptom relief timeline, quality of life) would differentiate ZoomRx's offering.

4. Implications for ZoomRx

4.1 Research Priorities

  1. TREMFYA vs. Skyrizi GI perception study — disentangle SOV leadership from data-driven preference
  2. ICOTYDE launch tracking — awareness, trial, switching, dual-brand messaging effectiveness
  3. MSL effectiveness deep-dive — why does SOV leadership not convert to SDP effectiveness superiority?
  4. Stelara biosimilar impact tracker — how does biosimilar availability affect TREMFYA and Skyrizi class positioning?

4.2 BD Opportunities

  • ICOTYDE PET — J&J has flagged this as CRITICAL in planning docs; $250-350K opportunity (Rank 1 BD priority)
  • TREMFYA IMM PET Takeover — full immunology PET engagement
  • TREMFYA Global Proposal — SFE, MEE, Rx Integration across markets ($568K submitted March 2026)
  • GI-specific MSL effectiveness study — unique offering that addresses the SOV-effectiveness gap

5. Thesis Check

Thesis Status Evidence
1. TREMFYA → $10B+ peak (GI-dependent) ON TRACK $1.6B Q1 2026 (74% growth), IBD new-start leader
2. ICOTYDE will cannibalize derm EARLY SIGNAL Strong demand from day one, $300M outlook raise
3. Skyrizi SEQUENCE gap is biggest GI vulnerability ACTIVE 75% capture rate, but TREMFYA leading new starts
4. Stelara erosion catalyzes TREMFYA HAPPENING Imm segment -8.8%, sales force pivot underway
5. MSL engagement is durable moat NEEDS EVIDENCE SOV leads but SDP effectiveness trails

Back to TA Knowledge Hub | Home
